speaker: interviewer
text: September, 1995, and we are in Sydney, Australia. Camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about the village where your family was hiding-- where your family was staying.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was a-- I remember the wine growing area because we, as children-- and there were-- there must have been-- I must have had, by this time, quite a few of the local children. I do remember quite a few of the local children. We used to go picking grapes. And then they were put in these big vats which were actually sitting on top of the back of a truck. And then the-- we used to be put into the vat, and stomp onto the grapes in our-- in our feet, in our dirty feet. So-- and this was-- actually, we used to do this quite a lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the-- the guy we were living with, he was a farmer. And he used to chase the pigs around the backyard. And he used to cut their heads off-- or was it the chickens? There was this terrific squealing in the back as they caught this poor old pig and slaughtered it. And he used to hang these bits of meat in his, in his-- I suppose it was a [INAUDIBLE], a smoking room. Right? Had this very pungent aroma of pork and beef and things. But there was plenty of food. [INTERPOSING VOICES] Do you remember the name of the farmer?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. Nice guy. I spoke Hungarian, already, by that stage. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there fore?

speaker: interviewee
text: About a year. Until the liberation-- we were liberated by the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the food that you ate there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think I must have eaten-- I don't eat pork or shellfish or things like that. But I think I must have eaten it there. I remember that-- the taste of it, I think. I don't know, it's supposed to have a very characteristic taste, pork. And I think it was a very staple diet in the rural areas of Hungary. But there was plenty of food, which was just fantastic, because we had never had it. There wasn't anything in the way of-- a lot of grapes, obviously. [LAUGHS] But lots of food for the first time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had these two rooms, actually, with this-- with the floor which was made out of the earth. But it was-- it was OK. It was fine. I actually enjoyed it. I liked that part. It-- I wasn't-- I-- I remember that I wasn't nervous. I wasn't-- there was no-- there was no one there apart from us, a few other Jews, and the villagers, and so on. They weren't-- they all seemed to be sort of nice people. There weren't anybody in authority. There was no uniforms. There was no regulations. There was-- it was just like, I suppose, going on a-- on a holiday on a farm. It was really good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever listen to the radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: This happened later, because it all changed. The Germans actually came into-- into-- well, they didn't come. Somebody came into this village. And we were then taken to a court house-- I suppose the only-- I suppose it must have been the town hall, or something. Some officious looking building. And there was a guy sitting behind a desk, and I remember he pulled down my pants, and I was circumcised. So he said I wasn't-- my name wasn't [? Matera. ?] I was obviously a Jew. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So it all changed after a while. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we must have started hiding out again after that, because I remember, with Abraham Bilfelb, I-- I was lying on this-- in this one huge room which, again, must have been the room where they kept the wheat and stuff. And there was just one radio. They would used to have a program every morning which used to announce the prices of-- of vegetables and meat in Hungary-- in-- in Budapest, on that morning. It wasn't exactly Triple J [LAUGHS] FM. But I remember that, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were staying in the farmhouse, what work were your parents doing each day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they worked-- they worked in the fields as well, picking grapes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes, because we were all together. Yeah, they did. And they also used to have sort of communal meetings. Sometimes I used to go at night. I think must have been, like, on a Friday night. And they actually sat around the table and ate, which was-- this was before-- you know, this was the good parts. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Which I remember that. It was sort of more joyous. Although it still-- it still gave me the feeling of being-- how should I put it-- not quite actually being free. But I had the feeling that things were better. Although there were still a lot of secretive talks seemed to have been going on amongst the adults. But there were-- there must have been Friday night meetings. And I suppose they must have had some prayers, or something. Although plenty of wine, too. Oh, yes, they used to drink wine. Of course. Yeah. There was plenty of wine. [LAUGHS] But then that-- then it all seemed to change very quickly, with-- with the arrival of the-- what I assumed-- not that I knew what the hell was going on in the rest of the world, but I assumed that it was the Germans had then walked into-- into-- had occupied Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any specific days where you felt something had changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember this day being taken to the-- to this place and my pants being pulled down. And then my-- it all seemed to sort of cave in again. It seemed we were back to-- my father, again, was sort of stressed out and saying, well, we're going to be deported, he was saying, and we're going to go on this train again. He said we'd have to go back on the train. They were going to take us back on the train. So I have this thing about trains. Although I always often wanted to go on a long train trip. But I don't like going on trains.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were supposed to go back on the train, somewhere. Going back to-- to Poland. I think that what happened was that all the-- if there were-- if you were discovered, then you were then shipped, not back to the ghetto, but this time back-- this time to the concentration camp, Auschwitz or something. Did you know about the concentration camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew because this is-- when we were in the ghetto, we knew the next step was going to a place where you never came back from. I mean, I knew there was-- this place existed. And that's the other thing I remember was that I couldn't understand, when my father said there was a world outside-- like we had an Aunt in America-- I said, well, why can't she come and help us? I mean, why don't the other people come and help you? There was an article about the fact that they didn't bomb Auschwitz, the Allies never bombed Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Otherwise they would have got rid of the-- they would have killed a lot of people, but they would have got rid of the ovens, I suppose. I suppose it was politics.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your family had to plan an escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm not sure whether there were-- there was any way to escape any-- I think it was a more resignation of-- of going back. But then there was news started filtering about the Russians and the war coming to an end. There was-- there was-- this was '43, '44, I suppose.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was already-- there was some-- I mean, there was information coming through that, obviously, the war is-- was going to come to an end fairly soon, right? It was a question of survival until that happened. And that the Russians were coming from the east. And, actually, we were rescued by-- liberated by the Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: The people from-- these-- people in this-- this officious looking office building just left one day. And the next thing I remember was I was taken out of this barn where I was sitting and listening to the reports of the fruit prices. And there were a lot of sort of soldiers running around this little village. And they wore this very strange uniform. They didn't look like the Germans, because the Germans were very well-dressed and kept with high boots. These had ragged clothes and-- and they had these little caps. They didn't have actually-- it wasn't-- it wasn't so frightening because the caps were like-- like little caps, in the center. And they had these green-- greenish or beige-looking uniforms. But they had a lot of guns hanging around. And there was one guy running around the street with this enormous clock-- alarm clock-- strapped to his wrist. And they said that the-- the reason why-- they used to-- these were the Russian liberators, and-- and they had-- they had very little in the way of possessions, or food, or anything. And they used to take anything that they-- because they were the liberators, I suppose-- they took anything they could get their hands on. So they used to take anything. And this guy couldn't find a watch, so he took an alarm clock to be like the other soldiers. He strapped it to his wrist, this huge alarm clock. He had it on his left wrist.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- and the-- we met the-- the captain of the-- of the brigade, and I know he was Jewish, because he greeted us-- I mean, it was just-- now that I think of it, he was an incredible-- all the people seemed to be so glad to see this one guy, you know, who was there dressed in this cap-- peaked cap, right? Which was the official uniform of the Russian army.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were very disheveled looking lot. Apparently they-- they had to live by their wits' end, because they didn't have any supplies. They were so far away from mother Russia. And he was-- he was Jewish. And then all of a sudden, everything seemed to be so terrific. They-- they looked after us very well. I mean, they gave us things, and-- and all of a sudden it was-- it was just-- just so different. It was-- And my father said that he was-- he was very happy now. I couldn't understand why. I mean, I couldn't see anything different apart from different looking people running around carrying on. But he seemed to think that they were-- they were the ant's pants. I mean, he-- he got along with this captain very well because they were both army men. And he seemed to get along with him like-- like he was some long lost brother, or something. I couldn't quite figure out what the hell was going on because they were so chummy all of a sudden. And the people seemed to be-- you know, my mother seemed to be happier than she'd ever been, and it just seemed completely different. I couldn't quite figure out what the hell was going on. And I didn't understand what it meant, that they said, well, as far as they were concerned, the war was finished. I didn't know what that meant. Because I'd never experienced anything else. The war was war. It was there. It was there forever, I suppose. So I couldn't quite understand what that actually meant. But then they were very-- then they seemed to be very upset again because they hadn't heard about any of their relatives. And I think they tried to get-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm not sure whether they tried to get some sort of information out of-- but anyway, the news was bad, that there was no one left. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then we left Hungary, for some reason or other.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the army captain?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. He was a very nice guy. [LAUGHS] I've got a mental picture of him, actually. But I don't remember-- I suppose, he-- he looked very old to me, but I think now, when I look-- when I-- in my mind's eye, he must have been in his 20s, I would think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what month it would have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was-- it wasn't hot and it wasn't cold, so it must have been either spring or autumn.

speaker: interviewee
text: When was it? 1944, wasn't it? I think that's when the Russians came into Hungary. The war ended in '45 and they actually came from the east in '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: You would have been eight years old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Eight. I was eight years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sorts of gifts did they give you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They seemed to-- there wasn't so much in the way of gifts, but they were sort of-- people were-- were running around, sort of not knowing what the hell they were doing, or something. It wasn't so much material gifts, but it was just sort of a more joyous type of atmosphere. Remember I was telling you about the fact that they had these meetings on Friday night. But they were sort of more-- always more hush hush and-- and more subdued. This was a sort of an exuberance of freedom, a running around in the middle of the streets. And that's-- that's-- that's the impression that I have now in my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wish I could remember it better. It must have been a great time. It must have been a fantastic feeling for my parents, I would imagine. I mean, I suppose it's something that's indescribable, isn't it? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But to me it didn't seem to make that much difference, yet. [LAUGHS] Apart from the fact that I saw my parents were much happier. What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we ended up in Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been, again, on the train. That was the only way that was-- no, we stayed-- now just a second. Now we stayed a bit longer in Hungary. I think we went back to Budapest, and from there we went by train to Bucharest. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we stayed in Bucharest with-- we rented a room at the back of a dental surgery. The woman was a dentist. That's right, the woman was a dentist. Don't know why that was. And they had cushions. They had cushions like this on the settee.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one night, my parents had some friends over, you see? And they said to them, those are nice cushions. And I said, oh my father stole them. [LAUGHS] And my parents were very angry at me. Because I had told the truth. My father had stolen them from somewhere. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the [INAUDIBLE] very first time I ever remember my father being angry at me. Up until then, I was-- I had to be obedient, but this is the first time I ever remember him being angry at me as a person because I had told these people the truth that we had stole-- we had stolen these-- these cushions. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was very cold in Romania, in Bucharest. When the snow fell in winter, it came up to the-- to the window sills. You had to dig yourself out. And they had-- and they had trams-- they had two lots of trans, first and second class. And a lot of gypsies lived in Romania. And you had to-- if you were a gypsy you had to be in the back, and if you were a white person you were in the front. We always, just because we couldn't afford it-- it was cheaper-- we always used to ride with the gypsies in the back. And in summer it was very hot. The asphalt used to melt in the streets, it was so hot. And people used to drink their tea very hot. They would tip it into their saucer and sip it from the saucepan. And King Michael was on the throne. It was a new world. It was incredible. And I used to deliver letters to-- between a young man and his-- and his girlfriend, and he used to give me something for that. And my father would sell-- he started making a living selling cards-- playing cards. And he and I would walk down the street, and I would accost a person, as a young boy, and say do you want to buy-- in Romanian, because he didn't speak Romanian very well. And they thought it was quite interesting that an eight-year-old would accost someone, and they used to buy these-- these playing cards. I used to do this every day with my father. I wonder why he stole the cushions.

words.text: I wonder why he stole the cushions.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much did you sell them for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry? [LAUGHS] I don't know. But it must have been enough to-- to survive on, to pay the rent for this dentist, and to live. But it was a great place. Yehudi Menuhin came to give a concert-- a Jewish guy. I'd never heard of a Jewish guy giving a concert before. And it was a-- it was-- it was a main street, I suppose like any main street in a major town, and there was a big theater there. And he actually came to give a violin concert. I don't think we went, but I remember him coming because my father said, look, there's a Jewish-- world-renowned Jewish man is coming into town to give a concert. And I went to the main square with my parents when King Michael was standing at the thing waving to the crowd, when he was still on the throne. But then things changed. I think the Russians deposed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what was happening in the rest of Europe at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I was-- I suppose I wasn't terribly interested. [LAUGHS] But I remember my parents talking about what to do. I mean, they were very sad about the fact that they'd lost all of their family. There wasn't-- I still don't remember that many joyous things. it was-- I mean, I didn't particularly want to walk around the streets selling cards with my father as an eight-year-old. That didn't exactly thrill me to bits. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your mother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think she cleaned the house where we were staying at. That's how probably we got-- yeah, she used to clean the dental [INAUDIBLE] surgery. That's right, the surgery was in the front of the house. The woman lived there, and she rented the room to us, and she had her practice in that. It was quite a good area of-- where I imagine was a good part of Budapest. And I think she cleaned and house keeped. My father went with me when selling cards, and I used to deliver these letters to these lovers who the parents didn't want to have anything to do, I suppose like Romeo and Juliet. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you attend school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not yet. I hadn't been to school yet. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No. No, I hadn't gone to school yet. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Bucharest?

speaker: interviewee
text: About nine months, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then the Russians started coming in and taking over, and my father said it was time to start-- start somewhere new. So we-- he said it's time to go to America. But we couldn't get there for some reason from Romania, so he said the best thing would be go to Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we caught a train from Bucharest through-- [SIGHS] through Germany, actually. That was a fascinating thing, that the train went through Germany. That's-- I couldn't understand why how come we, as Jews, could go through-- because there were no borders, in those days. There were no borders in between the different countries.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was all in upheaval. There were people, I think, the different nation-- different nationalities we're grabbing as much as they could. And so the train actually went to Germany. And we ended up in Paris, and this is where I went to school for the first time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Must have been the end of '45, '46. I went to [INAUDIBLE] the first school, I suppose, for my equivalent age. And I don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me more about that train journey through Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just found it terribly odd, for some reason. Because-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because they said, look, it was only last year, or the year before, when-- when we were there, when, you know, when you couldn't travel at all. Oh, that's right. He said, look. Look how different it is. You know, you couldn't travel anywhere. And there you could actually get onto a train and travel without being whisked off the train and shot, deported, or whatever. It was just-- I think-- I think my parents actually marveled at this-- at this idea that this was now just so incredible. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I think they imparted this-- this to me, that-- how different things were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Paris, yeah, and we lived in a hotel for a year. It was not unusual to live in hotels. You actually-- it wasn't exactly the Regent. It was a little hotel on Boulevard Saint-Michel on the-- on the left bank that's right near the Sorbonne, which is the students' quarters. I've been there many times to have a look. And there was a little hotel [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] next to the university, and we had one room there. And it wasn't unusual, because there was a woman living in one of the other rooms that had been there for 25 years. So it was-- it was-- it was quite OK to live in-- in a hotel for all those years. And we lived in this one room. My father always used to be a very nappy dresser. He was a very handsome man. And I remember him getting dressed one day. He always used to put his pants on last. He'd put his shoes, coat, and tie, and everything. And he was fixing up his tie and he was walking down the stairs, but he had forgotten to put his pants on. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I went to school just around the corner from the Sorbonne. And the teacher was very strict. He had his fingers amputated during the first World War. And it was such a strict school that if he said, hands behind your back, if you-- if you were slow, he would come and bang you with his stump of his hand on your back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. But I learned to read and write in French. It was the first time I went to school. But I must have been sufficiently literate. I think my mother must have learnt-- taught me in Polish how to read and write so I could go to school and pick it up. Because I was-- obviously had enough marks to stay in the class. I wasn't thrown out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But Paris was terrific. This is really-- I mean, that was just great. I'll always love Paris, ever since. I went back many times, whenever I could.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this stage did you know what had happened to the Jews in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [INAUDIBLE] We knew we'd lost all our family and we only had one sister left who lived in-- in America. And this is what our aim was, to get there. And this--

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was one part of France which I disliked tremendously. There was a-- a-- you had to go to this office near the Place de la Concorde [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for refugees to fill out papers and try to get-- immigrate somewhere, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: And you have to sit there for hours and hours and hours, in the sense that, you know, because you just have to wait your turn. And by this time I was-- I was now no longer that sort of terribly obedient child. And I went crazy sitting there waiting hours, after hour, after hour. And to this day, I can never stand in a line, in a queue, waiting for tickets or anything. I go quite-- quite peculiar. If you put me in a line, by the time I get to the front, I'm so sarcastic to the person that I just can't do it. Because I spent weeks, and weeks, and weeks, standing-- sitting there without anything to do. You can imagine, it's just a terrible thing to just stand there, or sit there, with your parents, with nothing to do, waiting, and waiting, and waiting. And that's not so important, but this is what-- part of the things that we used to do to try and get papers to go to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience antisemitism in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. I can't remember any. But there was one tragedy that happened to the-- not to us. But my father then used to sell cigarettes, because the currency in those days was nylon stockings and cigarettes. For cigarettes you could get into the best restaurants that francs couldn't buy. I don't know why, but obviously people smoked. And my father used to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] get cigarettes from one country and bring them across to the other. And they used to-- they had them in big boxes, and he was traveling on top of the truck and the truck tipped over. And his partner, who was this guy who was the father of this-- had escaped-- fell off and the box fell on top of him and killed him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he survived the war, but he didn't survive that. But my father did. I mean, he-- this is how he made-- I think this is how he made a living, selling cigarettes. At least this time I didn't have to walk around with him. [LAUGHS] I went to school. When did you go to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to America in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1947. Yes, 1947. We wanted to immigrate, but there was a big-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a seven-year wait for Polish migrants to the United States. And because we had relatives there, we thought, if we went there, and once we were in America, they would leave us there. But that didn't eventuate. But we went by ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: The SS America. And it was very stormy in the middle of the Atlantic. The waves used to go right over the-- it was a small ship. And I was never-- for three days, I was so seasick. It was unbelievable. Everybody was seasick, but I was terribly seasick. It was the first time I drank Coca Cola. It's a funny taste, actually. And we arrived in New York, and my father got put into Ellis Island because he probably didn't have the right papers, or something. My mother-- and then we were separated again. That's right, because I went to live with my aunt in Connecticut-- New London, in Connecticut, and my mother stayed in New York with another relative. But eventually my father got released from Ellis Island and there was all these negotiations about trying to get the authorities to allow us to stay in the United States. I went to school in America. In this little town in New London, Connecticut, I was the only-- the first person they'd ever seen who was a refugee. And they-- the Americans were very nice, because they had never seen a refugee before. I suppose, you know, I was just some different sod. And it was really terrific. They were very friendly. And again, this-- now, this again was a big transition because my aunt had a refrigerator. She had cars. They had milk in cartons. It was just a fantastic life. It was paradise. And to this day I love America. I should have stayed there. It was terrific.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks, Stephen.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of tape three of the interview with Stephen Muller. It's the 29th of September, 1995, and we're in Sydney, Australia.
